sentences.text: Good morning, Mrs.
Rappaport.
My name is Kurt, K-U-R-T, Lewin, L-E-W-I-N.
My name at birth was the same, but my middle name was Isaac, or in Hebrew, Yitzhak.
It would be I-S-A-A-C, or Y-I-T, like in Thomas, Z-H-A-K.
Yes.
I lived under assumed names as a Ukrainian, after my escape from the ghetto, as Roman or Roman Mytka.
R, like in Robert, O-M, like in Mary, A-N; Mytka-- M, like in Mary, Y-T, like in Thomas, K-A.
After moving on to Lublin-- to the liberated part of Poland, by the red army-- because of anti-semitism and killing of Jews, I assumed a Polish name.
I kept the same first name, which was Roman.
But I used the name Matkowski, which is M-A-T, like in Thomas, K-O-W-S-K-I.
And under this name, I served at the end of the Second World War, in the Polish army.
Any other names?
No.
Nicknames?
No.
I was born on February 28, 1925.
I am 71 years old.
In another few weeks, I will be 72.
I was born in Katowice, in Silesia.
K-A-T, like in Thomas, O-W-I-C-E.
That is a complicated question, because Silesia is on the borderline of Poland and Germany.
At certain times, it was Germany.
At other times, it was Poland.
It was Poland.
No.
Originally, the family started in Amsterdam, according to my knowledge.
I come from a rabbinical family, and most of my ancestors were rabbis.
And they moved from place to place, according to the rabbinical chair which they occupied at a given time.
My father.
Dr.
Ezekiel Lewin.
After taking his PhD at the Jagiellonian University in Krakow and competing his rabbinical studies, was ordained as an orthodox rabbi.
And his first rabbinical chair which he occupied was Katowice.
Very little-- I was a child.
I left Katowice when I was about three years old.
So my memories are rather vague.
I moved with my parents to Lwow.
I pronounce the name of the city in Polish.
It would be, probably, useful if I will indicate that the city in Polish is Lwow.
In German, it is [?
Numberg. ?] In Russian, it is [?
Lviv. ?] In Ukrainian, it is Lviw.
Lviw would be spelled L-V, like in Victor, I-W.
That's the way it's spelled and pronounced, but in Ukrainian.
Oh, yes.
Well, I have very few recollections from Katowice as a child.
But I distinctly remember my father being greeted by the community in Katowice.
And quite a number of people came to the railroad station to say goodbye to my parents.
I lived in Lwow on a street called [INAUDIBLE], 3rd of May, number 7.
It is the day where the Polish [INAUDIBLE], an equivalent of Congress, has accepted, a-- formally, a constitution-- a new constitution which is called the Constitution of 3rd of May.
And it is modeled, somewhat, on the American Constitution, or related.
You got me, but I would assume it's 1796 or '95.
I lived in a apartment house which was built, probably, at the turn of the century-- as Lwow was an administrative center of Austro-Hungary, of southern Poland.
It was a house quite similar to the ones you find in Vienna, or equivalent of Park Avenue, in New York.
Yes.
My father had his office there, his library.
And there were living quarters.
To my recollection, there were about seven rooms.
My father, my mother, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my younger brother was born there, at the time when we lived in Lwow, and shortly after we arrived.
And my youngest brother, who was born a few years later.
So this was the family.
Yes.
Yes.
We had, first of all, a housekeeper and a nanny for the children.
And in addition, every morning a secretary of my father-- who was provided by the Jewish community-- used to come and be there during office hours, equivalent of 9:00

That's a very difficult question the question-- because the tendency would be to relate it to the Jewish theological structure here, in the United States, which is orthodox, conservative, and reformed.
It was neither.

But after the Haskalah, there were movements in the Jewish community-- in Germany and in Austria, which eventually spilled into Poland-- to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] adapt the form of worship to different times-- namely, to the emancipation of Jews and no longer existence in ghettos.
You had, in Vienna, this movement-- in Leipzig, in Frankfurt.
And they called it enlightened movement or progressive movement.
In fact, these were orthodox congregations, but with a more formal form of worship.
And they were worshiping in, what they referred to, temples.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The community in Katowice was German-oriented.
And therefore, it was a reflection of the same movement in Germany.
The community in Lwow was more Vienna-oriented, and reflected the same movement in Vienna.
My father was an orthodox rabbi.
Oh, yes.
I-- I didn't have any direct relationship with the Hasidic movement, but I'm aware of what it was.
Hasidic movements stem from the days of Baal Shem Tov.
They were perverted-- and I use my word specifically and not by accident-- by subsequent evolution of Jewish sects which were headed by dynastic rabbinical families, like [INAUDIBLE], Belz, Sudmore.
And there were [?
Kumarna, ?] [INAUDIBLE], many, many.
Yes, they were everywhere.
But they were basically a minority.
The relationship was friendly, and the relationship was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] somewhat delicate because my family was a family of [INAUDIBLE].
I don't wish to go into a lengthy discussion of what [INAUDIBLE] means.
And this was a predominant trend in the Jewish community in Poland and in Lithuania.
That's a good question.
My father wore what was referred to in Austria, [INAUDIBLE].
This was a suit [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with pants, but a three-quarter jacket, double-breasted.
It is in-- not a rabbinical outfit because rabbis, you see, in orthodox community wore a [INAUDIBLE].
This was a Western, but it was three quarters.
Always, yes, and the yarmulke.
Always-- a yarmulke at home, but a hat on the street.
No question about it.
She would wear a hat.
My mother used to dress in the Western fashion, as most other women did.
Certainly not a sheitel, which was considered a barbaric sort of a custom in the community at large.
No.
Well--
I wore normal clothing.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt, as a child, quite oppressed by the fact that I was in the public eye.
My father was a very prominent man.
So I was known, and I was observed.
And I resented it.
I tried not to embarrass my father, but I wouldn't necessarily cover my head unless I was in some public function also.
We spoke, in Katowice, German-- because this was a primarily German Jewish community.
And my first language, therefore, was German.
In Lwow, I spoke Polish.
Most Jews in southern Poland and places-- Krakow, Tarnow, Rzeszow, Lwow-- used Polish.
And the community spoke a beautiful Polish.
I attended services, usually, on Saturdays and on holidays.
My father attended services every day.
The usual cycle of Jewish holidays, which you'll find in every traditional Jewish home.
As a child, I found the-- [HEBREW]-- I use a Hebrew expression, which means the new years.
Yom Kippur [INAUDIBLE], rather oppressive.
And I liked, very much, the [NON-ENGLISH], which means the three pilgrimage holidays-- Passover, Shavu'ot, Sukkot.
We had a traditional Saturday at home, which I loved very much.
As my father was a prominent man and was under continuous pressure of meetings and duties, so it's only on Friday and Saturday that we have-- had him to ourselves.
Well, the usual.
My father used to come from the synagogue.
And as he was orthodox, he used to put, actually, a silk [INAUDIBLE] and a girdle [INAUDIBLE].
And used to pray and sing the welcoming hymns, which are traditional.
And we had the kiddush.
Not really.
I had little interest in it.
And not only that, as I have pointed out, I had a slight resentment because the pressure's on me.
And when I do other things, which I experienced in the outside world, so I didn't know where I am.
Consequently, I kept relatively neutral-- friendly and neutral.
I liked the Shabbat because of the atmosphere of the house, which was a traditional, cultivated Jewish home.
The housekeeper.
My mother was a president of many women's organizations, and so forth.
And the pressures on her were equally great as on my father, because the wife of the rabbi had quite a number of social and-- duties.
The meal consisted, usually, what you would find in every Jewish home in those days-- namely, of gefilte fish, and soup, and meat, and some dessert, and that's it.
Oh, there was always a guest.
There was one guest, was a very strange man because he was always, on Fridays, invited to the rabbi's house, traditionally.
So as there were two chief rabbis who shared the rabbinical chairs, one Friday night he spent with one family, and one Friday night was another one.
His name was Senator Bodek Jakub Bodek.
And he was a senator.
He was elected senator, representing the Jewish community in the Polish [INAUDIBLE], or senate, which is the equivalent of the Congress.
He was the director of the railroad district for the Austro-Hungarian government.
He was engaged to a young lady who died very shortly before the wedding.
And the man dedicated his life to charity.
And all his life, he remained single, and just simply addressed himself to servicing the poor, the people in need.
And therefore, traditionally, the rabbis greeted him on Friday.
And he was always a guest, either at my father's home or the other rabbi.
Oh, the housekeeper.
My mother was too busy.
Oh, no.
[LAUGHS] That is my mother.
You see, I have to stress that my father was an orthodox rabbi.
And consequently, all the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] restrictions and way of life which you would find in any Jewish orthodox home were present in my home.
No question about it.
The restrictions of kashrut and things like that didn't bother me too much.
I just had to behave in-- in a way, in a fashion that people wouldn't come to my father saying that I'm behaving like a Gentile child, and get into fights and things of that sort.
What do you mean by that?
Position of the rabbi in the Old World was entirely different than it is here in the United States.
These people were law-givers.
They administered law, because Jews refrain from going to courts.
They were spiritual leaders of the community, and very highly-regarded and respected.
Rarely, if ever, they were paid a very generous salary.
So they-- their lifestyle was relatively modest.
My impression is, is my father was a chief rabbi of a very large, very wealthy community.
He was paid-- looking at it from my perspective today-- adequately.
We were not in a need.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But my impression is that we could not afford too many luxuries.
However, my mother was a rather wealthy, because her father was an estate owner-- very large estate.
Primarily a residential.
Well, I went first to public school in Lwow.
And the public school system, which was left over by Austro-Hungary, was excellent.
And the school's name was Adam Mickiewicz, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a very famous Polish writer and poet-- more or less a contemporary of Byron and Pushkin.
Was a very fine school.
There I finished six grades.
The composition of the city has, in a way, determined the composition of children in the school.
One third was Jewish.
One third was Polish, and one third was Lithuanian or Ukrainian.
No.
--in the classes as well?
No, no.
How about the staff, the teachers?
Were they all Polish or-- All Polish.
Polish primarily.
No.
Jews were teaching in some schools, but it was not common to see many Jewish teachers in the public school system.
I was a curiosity because, first of all, my father was a leader of men and leader of the community and well-known in Jewish and non-Jewish circles.
Secondly, because of the position of my father, I did not attend school on Saturdays which, in a way, was a handicap.
Because all through my schooling, I missed Saturdays.
I was not a very good student.
And I was always observed.
So my memories are mixed.
By the teachers and other students.
Because being my father's son, I was expected to perform in a competent and remarkable way, which I wasn't able to do.
End of story.

After religion was introduced to the curriculum in the second grade, the Roman Catholics were instructed by a Roman Catholic priest, the Lithuanian by a Greek Catholic priest, and the Jews by a teacher of religion.
So the minute religion was introduced, you had first signs of anti-semitism and friction between the kids.
The Poles antagonistic toward the Lithuanians, who were Greek Catholic, Byzantine Catholics, and both against Jews.
Fights, very simple.
Oh, many.
But it's a long story and, frankly, not interesting, because this was a way of life.
A Jew had to defend himself, or be beaten up.
I was--
No, because I used to beat up others.
I was rather a scrappy little boy, and knew how to defend myself.
And occasionally, I ran into difficulties.
But I was exposed to anti-semitism and direct attacks, since the age of, probably, eight.
It's way of life.
Oh, yes.
Jewish boys played together, yes.
After the introduction of religion, it ended.
I had some--
The usual-- played ball.
With boys.
And we used to go to the park.
And the normal, [INAUDIBLE] to wander through the city, which was very interesting because very beautiful city with many things to offer.
And that's all I can tell you.
It was nothing unusual.
Yes.
Until religion was introduced.
Eight, yeah.
Yes.
After, no, because in the introduction of religion, was immediately segregated the children and introduced barriers.
Yes.
My father tried very hard to arrange for me a Hebrew religious education.
But I did not respond to it, and finally my father-- being a very intelligent man-- realized that by forcing me, he only increased the resentment.
The result was that I had no religious education.
I was free.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, yes.
No question about it.
Oh, I was trained by a very pleasant young teacher.
And I could sing my [INAUDIBLE].
And--
It's somewhere at the end of February-- I couldn't tell you it.
I don't remember any longer, but I used-- I read the [INAUDIBLE], and I also read from the Torah, which I still am able, occasionally, to do.
And then I-- there was a reception in my house, and I delivered a discourse on [INAUDIBLE].
Namely, there are, as you know, there are [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, by all means.
